content.text: JSON mode and structured outputs are mechanisms for getting predictable, machine-parseable responses from LLMs. Without them, you rely on regex or brittle string parsing. With them, you receive valid JSON — and in the strictest case, JSON that is guaranteed to match a specific schema. Three different approaches serve different trade-offs: JSON mode (valid JSON, no schema guarantee), OpenAI Structured Outputs (schema-guaranteed, OpenAI only), and the

attrs.alt: Three structured output methods compared: JSON Mode (valid JSON, no schema guarantee), Structured Outputs (schema-valid, OpenAI only), instructor (validated Pydantic model, any provider); decision guide

content.text: instructs the model to always emit syntactically valid JSON. The model will never produce truncated JSON or mixed text-and-JSON. You still need to describe the expected schema in the prompt — the API only enforces parsability, not structure.
Parse the returned dict through a Pydantic model to validate types and catch missing keys before your application logic runs.

and later) passes the Pydantic model's JSON Schema to the API. The model is constrained server-side to produce output that exactly matches the schema — you get a fully validated Pydantic instance with no chance of schema mismatch.

from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional, Literal
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()


class Ingredient(BaseModel):
    name: str
    amount: float
    unit: Literal["g", "ml", "tsp", "tbsp", "cup", "piece"]


class Recipe(BaseModel):
    title: str
    servings: int
    prep_time_minutes: int
    ingredients: list[Ingredient]
    steps: list[str]
    difficulty: Literal["easy", "medium", "hard"]


def extract_recipe(recipe_text: str) -> Recipe:
    completion = client.beta.chat.completions.parse(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "Extract the recipe into structured data."},
            {"role": "user", "content": recipe_text},
        ],
        response_format=Recipe,     # Pass the Pydantic class directly
    )
    return completion.choices[0].message.parsed   # Always a Recipe instance


recipe_text = """
Chocolate Mug Cake (serves 1, 5 min)
Mix 4 tbsp flour, 4 tbsp sugar, 2 tbsp cocoa, 1 egg,
3 tbsp milk, 3 tbsp oil. Microwave 3 min. Easy.
"""

recipe = extract_recipe(recipe_text)
print(recipe.title)          # Chocolate Mug Cake
print(recipe.ingredients)   # [Ingredient(name='flour', amount=4.0, unit='tbsp'), ...]
instructor: Validated Structured Output for Any Provider
library wraps any LLM client and uses function calling or JSON mode under the hood to extract a Pydantic model. If the model returns invalid JSON or a schema-violating object,
automatically retries, including the Pydantic validation error in the retry prompt so the model can self-correct.

import instructor
from openai import OpenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional

# Patch the client
client = instructor.from_openai(OpenAI())


class ContactInfo(BaseModel):
    full_name: str = Field(description="Person's full name")
    email: Optional[str] = Field(None, description="Email address if present")
    phone: Optional[str] = Field(None, description="Phone number with country code")
    company: Optional[str] = None


def extract_contact(text: str) -> ContactInfo:
    return client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        response_model=ContactInfo,   # instructor handles the rest
        max_retries=3,                # retry up to 3 times on validation failure
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "Extract contact information."},
            {"role": "user", "content": text},
        ],
    )


contact = extract_contact(
    "Reach out to Sarah Connor at sarah@example.com or +1-555-0100. She works at Skynet Inc."
)
print(contact.full_name)  # Sarah Connor
print(contact.email)      # sarah@example.com
print(contact.company)    # Skynet Inc.

import instructor
import anthropic
from openai import OpenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel


class Sentiment(BaseModel):
    label: str   # positive / negative / neutral
    score: float  # 0.0 to 1.0
    reasoning: str


# With Anthropic Claude
anthropic_client = instructor.from_anthropic(anthropic.Anthropic())
result = anthropic_client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=256,
    response_model=Sentiment,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "I absolutely love this product!"}],
)
print(result.label, result.score)   # positive 0.97


# With Ollama (OpenAI-compatible endpoint)
ollama_client = instructor.from_openai(
    OpenAI(base_url="http://localhost:11434/v1", api_key="ollama")
)
result = ollama_client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama3.2",
    response_model=Sentiment,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Shipping was three weeks late."}],
)
print(result.label, result.score)  # negative 0.85

import instructor
from openai import OpenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Literal

client = instructor.from_openai(OpenAI())


class BugReport(BaseModel):
    title: str
    severity: Literal["critical", "high", "medium", "low"]
    affected_component: str
    reproduction_steps: list[str] = Field(
        min_length=1, description="Ordered list of steps to reproduce."
    )
    expected_behavior: str
    actual_behavior: str
    workaround: str | None = None


def parse_bug_report(raw_text: str) -> BugReport:
    return client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        response_model=BugReport,
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "Parse the bug report into structured form."},
            {"role": "user", "content": raw_text},
        ],
    )


report = parse_bug_report("""
CRITICAL: Login page crashes when password contains emoji 🔑.
Steps: 1. Go to /login 2. Enter emoji in password field 3. Click Submit.
Expected: Login or error message. Actual: 500 Internal Server Error.
Auth service is affected. No workaround found.
""")
print(report.severity)              # critical
print(report.reproduction_steps)   # ['Go to /login', 'Enter emoji...', 'Click Submit']
injects format instructions into the prompt and validates the response.
is the preferred LCEL approach, using function calling internally.

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import PydanticOutputParser
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


class MovieReview(BaseModel):
    title: str = Field(description="Movie title")
    year: int = Field(description="Release year")
    rating: float = Field(description="Rating out of 10")
    summary: str = Field(description="One-sentence summary")


# Approach 1: with_structured_output (preferred for LCEL)
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)
structured_llm = llm.with_structured_output(MovieReview)

result: MovieReview = structured_llm.invoke(
    "Review: Inception (2010) is a mind-bending thriller about dreams within dreams. 9/10."
)
print(result.title, result.rating)  # Inception 9.0


# Approach 2: PydanticOutputParser in a prompt chain
parser = PydanticOutputParser(pydantic_object=MovieReview)
prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "Extract structured data from the review.\n{format_instructions}"),
    ("human", "{review}"),
]).partial(format_instructions=parser.get_format_instructions())

chain = prompt | ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini") | parser
result2 = chain.invoke({"review": "The Matrix (1999) — a revolutionary sci-fi classic. 10/10."})
print(result2.year)  # 1999
LCEL chains with structured output step
: The model returns valid JSON but may use different key names than expected. Always describe the exact keys in the system prompt and validate with Pydantic before accessing fields.
Not mentioning JSON in the prompt for JSON mode
: The API requires that the word "JSON" appear somewhere in the messages when using JSON mode. Omitting it raises an error.
. If the schema is too complex for the model to satisfy, retries exhaust without success.
: Pydantic fields without a default are required. If the text might not contain all fields, use
to prevent validation failures on partial data.
: OpenAI Structured Outputs does not support
JSON mode (also called structured outputs) is a mechanism for making LLM APIs return

rather than free-form prose. When enabled, the model is constrained at inference time to only produce tokens that are valid JSON, preventing the truncated braces, text-before-JSON preamble, and mismatched keys that make raw LLM outputs brittle in production.
Three distinct mechanisms exist with different guarantee levels: (1)
) guarantees syntactically valid JSON but does not enforce a specific schema; (2)
passes a Pydantic model's JSON Schema to the API and enforces schema compliance server-side; (3) the

library wraps any provider's client, uses function calling or JSON mode under the hood, and adds automatic retry with Pydantic validation error feedback. Understanding which mechanism applies — and its guarantees — prevents subtle production bugs.
Without structured outputs, parsing LLM responses requires fragile heuristics: regex to extract a JSON substring from surrounding prose, try-except blocks around

, and manual checks for missing keys. On average, production LLM pipelines without structured outputs spend 30–50% of their engineering time on output parsing and error handling — work that structured outputs eliminate.
on the next because the prompt is interpreted differently. This causes
at runtime, downstream data corruption, and silent failures in batch jobs. Structured outputs with Pydantic validation catch these mismatches at the API boundary before they propagate.
The API guarantees syntactically valid JSON. You never need a try-except around json.loads() for parse failures — only for schema validation.
With OpenAI Structured Outputs or instructor, the response is already a validated Pydantic model instance. No dict access, no KeyError — just attribute access with type checking.

When the model returns a schema-violating object, instructor feeds the Pydantic validation error back into the next API call, letting the model correct itself. This handles ambiguous inputs that are hard to parse even for capable models.
The same Pydantic model works with OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, Ollama local models, Gemini, and others — swap the client, keep the schema.

Once the API returns a Pydantic model, your IDE provides autocomplete, mypy catches attribute typos at static analysis time, and runtime errors shift from 'wrong key name' to 'model changed schema' — a much easier error to catch.

The most common misconception. JSON mode only guarantees valid JSON syntax. The model may return completely different keys than described in the prompt, silently passing json.loads() but failing downstream.
The server-side schema enforcement is proprietary. You cannot use it with Claude, Gemini, or open models. For multi-provider pipelines, instructor is the portable alternative.
OpenAI Structured Outputs does not support dict[str, Any], Union with 3+ members, or recursive models. Complex schemas must be simplified or use instructor instead.
Each validation failure triggers a new API call. For a model that frequently fails complex schemas, you may pay for 2–3× the expected API calls. Always set max_retries and monitor retry rates.
Structured output JSON formatting adds tokens. A structured response might be 20–40% longer than an unstructured prose response, increasing cost per call.

If the input text genuinely does not contain the required field (e.g., asking for 'age' from text that never mentions it), the model must either return null or hallucinate. Design schemas with Optional fields for ambiguous cases.
Extracting entities (people, dates, amounts) from unstructured text into a database. Structured outputs ensure the INSERT columns match the response fields.
When your LLM is generating the response payload for a downstream API consumer. The consumer expects a specific JSON schema — structured outputs guarantee compliance.
Classifying a document and also returning the label, confidence, and explanation. JSON mode prevents the model from returning just a string label.
In LangChain LCEL chains or LlamaIndex pipelines where one step's output is another step's input. Structured outputs provide a typed interface between steps.
When NOT to use structured outputs:
For conversational responses where natural language is the expected output format (chat assistants, content generation).
For very complex schemas (deeply nested, many Union types) with small models — the model will struggle to comply and instructor's retries will exhaust quickly. Simplify the schema.
For streaming responses where you need to display partial output to the user — structured output requires the full response before parsing.

A common production pattern is batch extraction from a corpus of documents: receipts, contracts, research papers, support tickets. Here is a complete pipeline that processes a batch with rate limiting, retry handling, and structured output validation.

import instructor
from openai import OpenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional
import time
import json
from pathlib import Path


class InvoiceData(BaseModel):
    invoice_number: str
    vendor_name: str
    total_amount: float
    currency: str = Field(default="USD")
    line_items: list[dict]          # [{"description": str, "amount": float}]
    due_date: Optional[str] = None  # ISO 8601 date or null
    is_paid: bool = False


class ExtractionResult(BaseModel):
    success: bool
    data: Optional[InvoiceData] = None
    error: Optional[str] = None
    retries_used: int = 0


client = instructor.from_openai(OpenAI())


def extract_invoice(text: str, max_retries: int = 3) -> ExtractionResult:
    start_retries = max_retries
    try:
        data = client.chat.completions.create(
            model="gpt-4o-mini",
            response_model=InvoiceData,
            max_retries=max_retries,
            messages=[
                {
                    "role": "system",
                    "content": (
                        "Extract invoice data from the provided text. "
                        "Return null for fields not present in the text. "
                        "Convert all amounts to float (e.g., '$1,234.56' -> 1234.56)."
                    ),
                },
                {"role": "user", "content": text},
            ],
        )
        return ExtractionResult(success=True, data=data)
    except Exception as e:
        return ExtractionResult(success=False, error=str(e))


def process_batch(
    documents: list[str],
    output_path: Path,
    requests_per_minute: int = 60,
) -> list[ExtractionResult]:
    results = []
    min_interval = 60.0 / requests_per_minute

    for i, doc in enumerate(documents):
        t_start = time.monotonic()
        result = extract_invoice(doc)
        results.append(result)

        # Simple rate limiter
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - t_start
        if elapsed < min_interval:
            time.sleep(min_interval - elapsed)

        if (i + 1) % 10 == 0:
            print(f"Processed {i + 1}/{len(documents)}: "
                  f"{sum(r.success for r in results)} succeeded")

    # Write results to JSONL
    with open(output_path, "w") as f:
        for r in results:
            f.write(r.model_dump_json() + "\n")

    return results


# Example usage:
docs = [
    "Invoice #INV-2024-001 from Acme Corp. Total: $1,250.00. Due: 2024-02-15. Item: Software License $1,250.00",
    "Vendor: Global Supplies Ltd. Invoice 99842. Amount Due: EUR 450.75. Payment terms: Net 30.",
]
results = process_batch(docs, Path("/tmp/extracted_invoices.jsonl"))
print(f"Success rate: {sum(r.success for r in results)}/{len(results)}")
Always describe the schema in the system prompt even with Structured Outputs:

Server-side enforcement guarantees the shape, but the model still needs to understand what to fill in each field. A system prompt that says 'extract amount as a float in USD' produces better results than relying on the schema field name alone.
Use Optional for fields that may not be present:
A field declared as str (required) will cause the model to hallucinate a value if it isn't in the text. Optional[str] = None signals that null is the correct answer for missing information.

Fields that should be one of a fixed set of values (sentiment: 'positive'/'negative'/'neutral') should use Literal[...] not str. This both constrains the model's output and provides clear documentation.
Try inputs that are deliberately ambiguous, missing fields, or have unusual formatting. Verify the model returns null for missing fields rather than inventing values.
Log all validation errors and retry counts:
A high retry rate on a specific schema field indicates the prompt or schema needs clarification. Track this as a metric and iterate on prompt wording.
For deterministic schema extraction tasks, temperature=0 reduces variance and improves schema compliance. Reserve higher temperatures for generation tasks.

Structured output overhead comes from two sources: (1) additional tokens in the request (schema description or JSON Schema encoding in the API call) and (2) retry API calls when validation fails. Empirically, instructor with gpt-4o-mini on well-designed schemas has a retry rate of 2–5% for moderate schemas, rising to 20%+ for schemas with many constraints.

For high-volume batch processing (>10,000 calls/day), prefer OpenAI Structured Outputs over instructor when on OpenAI — it eliminates retries entirely. For Anthropic/Ollama, instructor with a simple schema and max_retries=2 is the most cost-effective approach.

If user-provided text is inserted directly into the messages, an attacker can inject content that corrupts the JSON structure in JSON mode. Always use the instructor/Pydantic layer as it re-validates the response regardless of what the model returns.

Structured extraction often captures PII (names, emails, addresses). Ensure output schemas that touch PII comply with GDPR/CCPA data minimization principles — only extract what you need, not everything present in the document.
When you update a Pydantic model schema, cached responses (if any) may not match the new schema. Version your schemas and include the schema version in logs to make debugging easier.

A model WILL hallucinate values for required fields rather than return a validation error. Never mark a field as required if it might not be present in the input. Treat the extracted data as LLM output — not ground truth — and validate against a secondary source for high-stakes decisions.
JSON mode guarantees valid syntax, not schema:
The model may return different keys than described. Always validate with Pydantic after json.loads().
JSON mode (syntax) < instructor with retry (schema with recovery) < OpenAI Structured Outputs (server-enforced schema, OpenAI only).
Works with OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, and Gemini. The same Pydantic model works across providers.
Required fields that might not be in the input will cause hallucination. Design schemas defensively.
instructor auto-retries with Pydantic error as context:
The model sees its own validation error and self-corrects. Set max_retries to control cost.
Monitor retry rates as a quality metric:
High retry rates indicate schema-prompt mismatch that should be fixed, not masked by increasing retries.
Reduces variance and improves schema compliance for deterministic extraction. Reserve higher temperatures for creative generation.
